title: Endpoint Resource Utilization (CIM)
spl:
index=perf tag=endpoint metric_name="cpu_pct"
| bin _time span=5m
| stats perc95(value) as cpu_p95 by _time, host
| where cpu_p95>90
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Performance.CPU by Performance.host span=5m | sort - count
description: Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware ‚Äî use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).
value: Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware ‚Äî use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).
grandmaExplanation: Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware ‚Äî use DM when TAs map (field name varies by TA) ‚Äî so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: Prefer `mstats` for metrics indexes; join CIM `Performance.host` to CMDB tier.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Perfmon, Telegraf ‚Üí Performance CIM.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: Normalized performance metrics per `host`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Prefer `mstats` for metrics indexes; join CIM `Performance.host` to CMDB tier.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=perf tag=endpoint metric_name="cpu_pct"
| bin _time span=5m
| stats perc95(value) as cpu_p95 by _time, host
| where cpu_p95>90
```

Understanding this SPL

**Endpoint Resource Utilization (CIM)** ‚Äî Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware ‚Äî use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).

Documented **Data sources**: Normalized performance metrics per `host`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Perfmon, Telegraf ‚Üí Performance CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: perf.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=perf, tags. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where cpu_p95>90` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Performance.CPU by Performance.host span=5m | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Endpoint Resource Utilization (CIM)** ‚Äî Sustained high CPU on many endpoints may indicate cryptomining or ransomware ‚Äî use `Performance` DM when TAs map `cpu_load_percent` (field name varies by TA).

Documented **Data sources**: Normalized performance metrics per `host`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Perfmon, Telegraf ‚Üí Performance CIM. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Performance.CPU` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (CPU p95), Heatmap (host √ó time).
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, backups, or batch jobs can drive metrics outside normal bands ‚Äî correlate with change management windows.
